coredata.dc:title: One pot synthesis of intriguing fluorescent carbon dots for sensing and live cell imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Jana, Jayasmita
Ganguly, Mainak
Das, Bodhisatwa
Dhara, Santanu
Negishi, Yuichi
Pal, Tarasankar
coredata.dc:description: We report a simple one-pot synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) via modified hydrothermal (MHT) treatment of alkaline solution of dopamine and cysteine. These CDs (λ ex=320nm, λ em=390nm, and quantum yield ∼5.1%) are of ∼2–3nm in diameter. Further attempt of synthesizing CDs in some common water-miscible solvents ends up the fact that the MHT product from acetone medium is nonfluorescent. However, CDs, produced in aqueous medium, are so stable that they can be dried as a deliverable solid (WCD) without any alteration of fluorescing property if reversibly dispersed in water. Fluorescence of WCD is quenched selectively in acetone. Quenching occurs presumably due to the disruption of radiative recombination along with the hindrance in quantum confinement of the emissive energy traps to the particle surface. Successive quenching of fluorescence of WCD in different acetone concentration admixed in water paves the way to selective acetone sensing (LOD=8.75×10−7 M). The synthesized CDs (in aqueous medium) are cytocompatible and are efficient fluorescent probe for cell imaging. Only living cells are recognized exclusively from fluorescence imaging leaving aside dead cells, while cells are treated with CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Acetone
Sensing
Cytocompatible
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs in different water-miscible solvent medium
Effect of different solvents on WCD: selectivity of acetone sensing
Acetone sensing condition and detection limit
Effect of different precursor on sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs. (Inset: particle distribution diagram). (B) HRTEM image of CDs showing fringe spacing. (C) Wide range XPS spectra of CDs under fridge drying condition. (D) Narrow range XPS spectra of C1s showing the composition of carbon.

(A) Fluorescence spectral profile of the MHT products prepared in different solvents. (B) Fluorescence spectral profile of the product in different acetone concentration. (C) TEM image of MHT product (carbon sphere) in acetone at different positions. Condition: [DA]=4×10−5 M, [cys]=1.6×10−4 M, [NaOH]=0.1M, volume of solvent =2.5mL, λ em=320nm.
(A) Fluorescence spectral profile and (B) bar diagram of vacuum dried solid mass from 0.5mL CD after redispersion 3mL of in different solvents at λ em=320nm.
(A) Fluorescence spectral profile of CD in different molar ratio of water and acetone. (B) (I0−I)/I0 vs. [Acetone]/[water] and linear detection range of acetone at λ em=320nm.
Fluorescence spectral profile of CD in different water miscible solvents after addition of 0.1mL acetone at λ ex=320nm.

(A) TEM image of CDAc in different acetone concentration; molar ratio of CD and acetone is (a) 1: 5, (b) 1:3, (c) 1.1. (B) Wide range XPS spectra of CDAc under fridge drying condition. (C) Narrow range spectra of C1s showing the components of carbon.

(A) Fluorescence spectral profile of MHT product obtained from the precursors (a) catechol - cys in (1) aqueous solution and (2) acetone, (b) L-phenyl alanine- cys in (1) aqueous solution and (2) acetone, (c) L-tyrosine - cys in (1) aqueous solution and (2) acetone, (d) DA - cys in (1) aqueous solution and (2) acetone, (e) L-DOPA - cys in (1) aqueous solution and (2) acetone. Condition: [catechol/ L- phenyl alanine/ L- tyrosine/ DA/ L-DOPA]=4×10–5M, [cys]=1.6×10–5M. pH=12, λ em=320nm. (B) Schematic representation of the metabolic pathway of DA.
(A) Fluorescence spectral profile of 0.5mL CD′ after redispersion in 3mL different solvents. (B) Fluorescence spectral profile of CD′ in different molar ratio of water and acetone at λ em=320nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectral profile of CD in different molar ratio of DMSO and acetone. (B) (I0−I)/I0 vs. [DMSO]/[Acetone] and linear detection range of acetone at λ em=320nm. Control solution has been prepared by dispersion of the solid mass obtained after vacuum drying 0.5mL CD solution in 3mL DMSO.
(A) MTT Assay Results. (B) Micrograph of cells (a) excitation of CDs, (b) same frame with excitation of ethidium bromide, (c) DIC image of same frame, (d) merged image of all the frames.
Intriguing fluorescence of carbon dot solution is utilized in live cell imaging. This fluorescence is quenched exclusively in the presence of acetone.

head.ce:title: One pot synthesis of intriguing fluorescent carbon dots for sensing and live cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Furman University, Greenville, SC 29613, United States

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Science & Technology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur 721302, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Science & Technology, Indian Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Tokyo University of Science, Tokyo 1628601, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Chemistry, Tokyo University of Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a simple one-pot synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) via modified hydrothermal (MHT) treatment of alkaline solution of dopamine and cysteine. These CDs (λ ex=320nm, λ em=390nm, and quantum yield ∼5.1%) are of ∼2–3nm in diameter. Further attempt of synthesizing CDs in some common water-miscible solvents ends up the fact that the MHT product from acetone medium is nonfluorescent. However, CDs, produced in aqueous medium, are so stable that they can be dried as a deliverable solid (WCD) without any alteration of fluorescing property if reversibly dispersed in water. Fluorescence of WCD is quenched selectively in acetone. Quenching occurs presumably due to the disruption of radiative recombination along with the hindrance in quantum confinement of the emissive energy traps to the particle surface. Successive quenching of fluorescence of WCD in different acetone concentration admixed in water paves the way to selective acetone sensing (LOD=8.75×10−7 M). The synthesized CDs (in aqueous medium) are cytocompatible and are efficient fluorescent probe for cell imaging. Only living cells are recognized exclusively from fluorescence imaging leaving aside dead cells, while cells are treated with CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have created immense interest to scientists since their discovery out of electrophoretic analysis and subsequent purification of fluorescent carbon nanotube fragments [1]. Generally, CDs attain dimension of about 10nm. They are often compared with known quantum dots (QDs). CDs are more biocompatible and less cytotoxic [2] than the well known QDs. CDs posses different intrinsic structures e.g. carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [3], carbon nanodots (CNDs) [4] and polymer dots (PDs) [5] etc. CQDs and some CNDs possess distinct lattices, whereas other CNDs and PDs generally have amorphous nature. Several techniques have been adopted for the synthesis of CDs [6–10]. Most of the techniques include hydrothermal treatment [11], solvothermal treatment [12], microwave pyrolysis [13], plasma treatment [14], laser ablation/passivation [15], electrochemical method [16] etc. The prepared CDs need further surface passivation to show better emissive property with higher quantum yield. The photoluminescence/fluorescence property of CDs is quite complicated due to quantum size effect [17], triplet carbenes at the zigzag edges [18], radiative recombination of the excitons [19] and the molecular state (as for organic dyes). Also the factors those affect the energy states (HOMO and LUMO) and the band gap between the energy states generally and directly affect the emissive nature of CDs. The optical properties of CDs are the consequences of either combination or competition amongst these factors. The emission mechanism of CDs may change depending upon the nature of precursor compounds and also on the CD preparation procedures [20]. Now CDs are widely employed in the field of drug delivery [21], biomedicine [22], bioimaging [23,24], optical imaging [25] sensing [26] and certain optical devices [27]. Bioimaging or cell imaging is an important application of CDs. Among the fluorescent agents for optical cell imaging/ bioimaging [23,24,28]. CDs prove to be one of the best suited candidates due to their ready aqueous solubility, physicochemical and photochemical stabilities, eminent optical performance including non-blinking behavior, and significantly superb biocompatibility.

Acetone is a common organic solvent, miscible with water in all proportions. Commonly it is used in nail polish remover, varnish removers, adhesives, plastics and paints, and used to make other chemicals such as acetylene. Low level of acetone is present naturally in the human body, plants, trees and volcanic gases also [29]. Higher amount of acetone, because of its efficient solubilizing property; can be harmful in human body. Presence of higher concentrations of acetone in human body is reported to be produced during fasting or crush dieting. When carbohydrate intake becomes low, ketosis occurs which in turn produces high amount of acetone in our body. Acetone exposure may cause skin dryness, dermatitis, throat and eye irritation etc. Acetone, if present in humans, can be measured from the breath, blood, and urine etc. Also a noninvasive breath acetone detection makes the way of the detection of type-I diabetes and helps to arrange the dose of insulin. Hence, acetone detection in blood sample is a good alternative measure for pathological analysis [30]. There are a good numbers of acetone vapor sensors like screen printed TiO2 nanoparticles [31], Si doped WO3 nanoparticles [32], thin-walled WO3 hemitubes [33], etc. Detection of acetone in solution is equally significant not only for health information purpose but also for analytical laboratories [34]. Acetone is considered as a hazardous waste. Decomposition of acetone produces toxic carbon monoxide [35]. Also its high flammability makes it hazardous. Presence of any kind of interference in terms of external particle or other water miscible solvents in aqueous phase reaction causes hazards and sometimes experimental failure. Hence it is important to use distilled and pure water for laboratory experiments. It is very difficult to detect any water miscible solvent in water while present in trace level. Porter et al. [36] reported the detection of the triplet state of acetone in solution by fast conventional flash techniques. Again there is other report of solvatochromogenic fluorimetry method for detection of water in acetone by Liu et al. [37]. Our strategy of acetone sensing is simple without any sophisticated instrumentation. It is a fluorometric technique exhibiting intriguing sensitivity as well as selectivity in solution phase. It is the first report, according to the best of our knowledge, of acetone sensing involving the quenching of fluorescence of carbon dots in solution.

Water soluble CDs with high emissive property have been prepared from a reaction between dopamine and cysteine precursors. They can effectively detect trace level of acetone in aqueous solution in terms of “turn off” fluorescence phenomenon. Moreover, the as prepared CDs have been found to be capable of working as an agent for selective live cell imaging ( Scheme 1).

All the reagents used throughout the experiment were of AR grade. Triple distilled water was employed during the experiment. Dopamine s (DA), l-DOPA (l-3,4- dihydroxyphenyl alanine), l-phenylalanine, l-tyrosine, cysteine (cys), N-acetyl cysteine and all solvents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was obtained from HiMedia Laboratories Pvt. Ltd. All the solid reagents were used in the experiment without further purification. All solvents were used after distillation. All glass wares were cleaned with freshly prepared aqua regia, rinsed with sufficient amount of distilled water, and dried well before use.

The fluorescence measurement was performed at room temperature using a LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, Waltham, MA). The samples were taken in a quartz cuvette of 1cm path length for fluorescence measurement. Absorbance was measured using Evolution 201 UV spectrometer, Thermo Scientific, United States. The samples were taken in a quartz cuvette of 1cm path length for absorbance measurement. XPS analysis was carried out with a VG Scientific ESCALAB MK II spectrometer (U.K.) equipped with a Mg Kα excitation source (1253.6eV) and a five-channeltron detection system. The samples were fridge dried before measurement. FTIR studies were carried out with a Thermo-Nicolet continuum FTIR microscope. The sample in solution phase was taken for measurement. TEM analysis was performed with a H-9000 NAR instrument, Hitachi, using an accelerating voltage of 300kV. The samples in solution phase were drop-cast onto a carbon-coated copper grid, and the grid was air dried at room temperature (25°C) before loading into the microscope. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured with Easy lifeR (Optical Building Blocks Corporation) equipped with a 295nm LED excitation source. The samples were taken in a quartz cuvette of 1cm path length for fluorescence measurement.

Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared from DA and cys. In the typical synthesis 25μL DA (5×10−3 M) and 100μL cys (5×10−3 M) were mixed together and the mixture was diluted to 3mL using triple distilled water taken in a screw cap test tube (final concentration of DA in 10−5 M order). The solution was made alkaline by adding NaOH (10−1 M). Then a screw capped test tube was kept in front of 200W bulb (Philips India) at a distance of 3cm for 6h. The whole system was kept in a 1ftx1ftx1ft closed wooden box. This method of heating a reaction mixture is reported as modified hydrothermal (MHT) [38] reaction. After 6h of reaction, a turbid yellow colored solution was obtained. Then the solution was centrifuged at 16,000rpm for 20mins to obtain a clear pale yellow solution.
For preparation of CDs in different solvents, the whole procedure mentioned above was performed in different solvents.

The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was measured according to a reference point method [39]. Quinine sulfate in 0.1m H2SO4 (literature quantum yield 0.54 at 360nm) was used as the standard reference. The quantum yield of a sample (sl) was measured with respect to the known quantum yield of the standard reference (st) using the following formula,
Φ
s
1
=
Φ
s
t
(
F
s 1
/
F
s
t
)
(
η
2
s
1
/
η
2
s
t
)
(
A
s
t
/
A
s
1
)

where Φ is the QY, F is the fluorescence intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical density. The subscript “st” refers to standard with known QY and “sl” for the sample.

An aliquot of 0.5mL of the as prepared CD solution was dried in vacuum to remove the water content completely. Then the dried product (WCD) was redispersed in 3mL acetone. This solution was nonfluorescent. However, when water was added, fluorescence intensity enhanced. Gradually the amount of water was increased and amount of acetone was decreased. This showed gradual enhancement in fluorescence of the prepared solution. Thus variation of the molar ratio of water:acetone led to acetone detection.

L929 fibroblast like cells (procured from NCCS, Pune) were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotic solution (Hi Media). For CD transfection assay cells were culture on poly-l-lysine coated cover slips with an initial seeding density of 10,000 cells slip−1.

In case of transfection assay live cell imaging was conducted on a Carl Zeiss inverted fluorescence microscope. Two filters (365nm, 570nm excitation and 470nm, 610nm emission respectively) for fluorescence mode and differential interference contrast for bright field imaging was employed. Six hours before the imaging the media of the cells was discarded and pH adjusted media mixed with CDs (100µLmL−1 of DMEM) was added. After six hours the media was discarded and cells for counter stained with ethidium bromide (SRL Chemicals). The cover slips were mounted on glass slides (Blue Star) before imaging.

For checking the cytocompatibility of the CDs 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was conducted. The test was conducted by seeding 5000 cells per well in a 24 well plate (Nunc). After 12h of seeding, different concentrations of CD (mixed with DMEM and pH adjusted to 7.4) were added. After 72h the media was discarded and the wells were washed twice and MTT solution (SRL Chemicals) again CD solution (mixed with DMEM and pH adjusted to 7.4) was added in a concentration of 50µgmL−1. After 4h of incubation at 37°C the MTT solution was discarded and 500µL of DMSO (Hi Media) was added. After shaking for 30min the absorbance was measured at 590nm in a plate reader (RMS Healthcare).

Highly fluorescent CDs have been synthesized employing dopamine (DA) and cysteine (cys) as precursor compounds in alkaline solution. It has been previously reported that DA is a good precursor for the preparation of carbon dots [11]. Also it is reported that the presence of cys enhances the emissive property of carbon dot as they may work as hetero atom (N and S atom) donor [40,41].

During the synthesis, variation of experimental condition reveals that pH=12 and a reaction time of 6h (Fig. S1A, and B) are the best suited experimental conditions for obtaining fluorescent CDs with highest emissive intensity. The role of pH is very important for our experiment. Generally alkaline medium causes enhancement in the emissive property of CDs. Free zigzag sites at the edge of the particle behave like fluorescence centers with carbene like ground state. Such states arise due to presence of surface defects in CDs. In alkaline condition, such state gets stabilized and the particles emit intensely. Under acidic condition, the free zigzag sites get protonated resulting in the destruction of the emissive sites associated with CDs. This ends up with loss in fluorescence intensity. However with the increase in the pH of the medium, deprotonation occurs which causes increased fluorescence of the particles due to the restoration of the emissive zigzag sites [42]. We have studied the fluorescence spectral profile through a month storing the solution at room temperature (25°C). No virtual change in the emission intensity and emission wavelength indicates the long term stability of the fluorescent particles (Fig. S1C). The fluorescence spectra have been recorded at different excitation wavelengths, from 200nm to 600nm. The highest fluorescence intensity has been obtained using 320nm excitation wavelength with an emission maximum at 390nm (Fig. S2). The excitation spectrum (when emission is at 390nm) also indicates that 320nm is the best suited excitation wavelength (Fig. S3A). The high energy excitation <488nm is generally attributed to intrinsic fluorescence from the CD core. Again the excitation wavelength often depends on the particle size and surface defects of the CDs. DA and cys are used as precursors for synthesis of the CDs. DA shows excitation at 266nm with emission at 320nm and cys has no inherent fluorescence. Hence the emission of the so produced CDs is distinctly different from the precursors. However, due to presence of cys, an amino acid, a polar shell of nitrogen containing group is formed around the carbon core in terms of primary amine groups. Presence of such polar group may cause such intense emission at a short wavelength [43]. The UV–vis spectrum shows the presence of two peaks at 256nm and 329nm (Fig. S3B). The quantum yield of the particles is ∼5.1% (quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 is used as standard for measuring quantum yield). The lifetime of the particles is found to be 1.4ns (Fig. S4). Generally the lifetimes of CDs are much higher like 7–12ns [39,41]. However, it is seen that surface passivated CDs show higher lifetime while bare CDs possess short lifetime [44]. The as synthesized CDs in the present case need no external surface passivation for stability and show enhanced fluorescence.

The TEM image clearly shows that the particles are homogeneously distributed and most of the particles lie within the diameter around ∼2–3nm ( Fig. 1A). The HRTEM indicates that the fringe spacing is 0.24nm (Fig. 1B) corresponding to the (1120) crystal phase of graphite [45]. In XPS spectra, presence of peaks at 284.6eV, 400eV, 164eV, and 532eV confirms the presence of C1s, N1s, S2p3/2, and O1s (Fig. 1C) [11]. The characteristic peak of sp2 hybridized carbon at 284.6eV denotes the presence of graphite structures. Those hybridized carbon atoms work as the fluorescence centers [46]. It is previously reported that on interaction of dopamine with cysteine a new compound [47] is generated with the formation of a six member ring containing S and N. Moreover the narrow range (Fig. 1D) spectrum of carbon shows that the carbon core consists of C–S and C–N interactions along with C–O and C–C interactions. Thus it may be concluded that under the MHT condition a carbon core is formed with S and N. Here cys works as the S and N atom donor. Only the –NH2 and acidic –COO− remain at the surface of the produced carbon dots (FT-IR data, Fig. S5(A)). The synthetic method is highly reproducible. We have repeated the experiment for five times to check the reproducibility. It always gives CDs of same size and shape (Fig. 1). Further venture of the preparation of CDs from precursors N-acetyl cysteine (NAC) and DA ends up with a product of less fluorescent intensity (CDLF) (Fig. S6). The quantum yield of the CDs from NAC and DA is quite low at the present condition; hence they are not considered for further experimentation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs in different water-miscible solvent medium
ce:section.ce:para: We have taken some water-miscible solvents like acetone, acetonitrile, N,N dimethyl formamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol (EtOH), isopropanol (iPrOH), methanol (MeOH) individually as the reaction medium instead of water for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots via our laboratory developed MHT. The precursors and other experimental conditions were kept unaltered. The product particles are termed as AcCD, AnCD, DfCD, DmCD, EtCD, IpCD and MeCD respectively. The emission spectrum of DmCD and DfCD shows another peak at ∼450nm. Both the solvents are strongly coordinating and presumably both of them form some new fluorescent species. To depict the exact mechanism further investigation is warranted. This experiment also reveals the fact that AcCD bears no significant fluorescence ( Fig. 2A). The decrease in the acetone concentration in the reaction medium causes gradual increase in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2B). The size of AcCD particles is much larger in size than the dimension of CDs obtained from aqueous and other water soluble non-aqueous solvents. TEM analysis shows AcCD particles are greater than 200nm (Fig. 2C). These particles should be termed as carbon spheres (CSs) instead of CDs. The TEM image (Fig. S7) shows different particle sizes in different solvents. AnCD particles are not definitely shaped mostly with diameter under 20nm. For DfCD, the range is ∼10nm but there are some smaller particles. DmCD particles lay under diameter range 10–20nm. EtCD particles are ∼20nm. IpCD particles are not well defined and MeCD, particles have the diameter ∼5nm. Presumably due to the large size (>200nm), AcCD bears continuous energy levels instead of discrete band gap [48]. For discrete band gap there occurs quantum confinement of emissive energy traps which causes fluorescence. Thus killing of quantum confinement makes AcCD nonfluorescent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different solvents on WCD: selectivity of acetone sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Now the CD solution, prepared in aqueous solution, is dried under vacuum (WCD). In WCD, different water-miscible solvents (acetone, acetonitrile, DMF, DMSO, EtOH, iPrOH, MeOH) are added separately to make different solutions. Generally the fluorescence intensity depends on the nature of the solvent like solvent viscosity, solvent refractive index, and coordinating ability of solvent etc. [49]. However in our experiment we have found that fluorescence intensity behaves anonymously after redispersion. Kumar et al. [50] have observed similar type of double peaks for polar aprotic solvents like ours. They concluded that, there is no clear dependence between emission peak wavelengths with fluorescence intensity for a particular excitation. When different solvents are added to the CD in the present case, the solvents immediately coordinate with the particles causing the surface passivation. Now the attachment depends on the polarity, steric effect and several other factors. Two peaks in emission spectra are generated from two emission centers. In DMSO, the electron deficient S atom interacts with the electron rich O− of carboxylate group of the CDs. Thus there occurs another effective interaction. This possibly generates two emission centers. However, further investigation is warranted for proper mechanism. However, acetone selectively quenches the fluorescence of the CDs by ∼19 times ( Fig. 3), while the other solvents virtually remain innocent in this context. This non fluorescent solution in acetone is termed as CDAc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acetone sensing condition and detection limit
ce:section.ce:para: WCD particles get easily dispersed in acetone with uniform shape. We do not find any type of precipitation in the solution even after one month. The absorption spectrum of CDs in acetone mixture was measured at different time with proper baseline correction without any stirring/shaking (Fig. S8A). As the absorption spectra remain virtually same, the possibility of precipitation does not arise. Particles are well dispersed and they do not settled down on long standing. Also the unaltered fluorescence intensity and TEM image obtained from dispersed solution speak against agglomeration. The quenching of the fluorescence of the CDs over time has been performed. It shows that maximum quenching of the fluorescence of CDs at a certain acetone concentration occurs within 90min (Fig.S8B). Then, fluorescence intensity becomes fixed and does not alter with time. The pH of the system shows an influence on the spectra of CDs in acetone (Fig. S8C). It is seen that with the increase in pH value quenching of fluorescence increases to some extent. It is due to the fact that with the increase in pH the interaction between the non-protonated surface groups and acetone increases. However at extremely low pH this interaction is not feasible at all as the surface functional group CDs are protonated hindering the emissive sites. Then the molar ratio of acetone and water is varied. It is observed that with the increase in the [acetone]:[water] molar ratio in the reaction medium, the fluorescence gradually decreases. Even at a [water]:[acetone]=1:8.75×10−7 (molar ratio), there occurs effective fluorescence quenching of the CD solution ( Fig. 4).

We have done the interference study in presence of other solvents (ethanol, methanol, DMSO, DMF, acetonitrile, 2-propanol) for quenching of the fluorescence of carbon dots by acetone. But the quenching of fluorescence of carbon dots by acetone is not affected virtually in the presence of interfering solvents in acetone water mixture. Fig. 5 depicts the selectivity of our proposed method.

Fluorescence of the CDs originates from the surface energy traps and also the radiative recombination of electron hole pair present at the surface. Thus any species that is good electron donor [51] can hamper the radiative recombination by the means of stabilization of hole on the surface by negatively charged electrons. Acetone is a very good electron donor; hence it causes sufficient quenching. The core of carbon dot is sufficiently large compared to the ligands surrounding the core. Thus acetone, a smaller molecule, can easily invade the ligand sphere; hence there happens a direct electron donation to the core region of the CDs. TEM image clearly depicts that upon increase in the acetone concentration in the system, the overall particle size (aggregation of CD particles with acetone molecules) increases. This also results in the gradual quenching of fluorescence intensity ( Fig. 6A). These large particles are neither the agglomeration of CD particles nor the synthetic artifacts. Actually when the CD particles are in acetone medium, the acetone particles interact with the functional groups present at the surface of the CD particles [52]. There exists a possibility of formation of an imine bond (FTIR data shows the presence of –C=N bond, Fig. S5B). As the CD concentration is fixed, the increase in the CDAc particle size with increased acetone concentration shows that at higher acetone concentration higher amount of acetones gets associated with CD particles forming overall large particles. Unusual aggregation in acetone causes thickness. Thus crystalline CDs with lattice fringe cannot be obtained. XPS results show the presence of ∼285eV, 400eV, 164eV and 432eV peaks representing C1s, N1s, S2p3/2 and O1s, respectively (Fig. 6B). The narrow range XPS spectra (Fig. 6C) shows that there is a slight shift in the C1s peak position from 284.6eV to 285.2eV indicating increase in the sp3/sp2 hybridization ratio. The decrement in sp2 nature of carbon causes the decreased emission intensity in terms of damping of radiative recombination of excitons [53]. CDAc has a much lower lifetime (0.9ns).

The decrease in the lifetime value can also be correlated with the red shift of the fluorescence peak [48]. This red shift of the emission maxima from CD to CDAc argues for energy loss to some extent during emission. It is also quite possible that there is some sort of energy transfer from the excited state of CD to excited state of acetone. Now, redispersion of the vacuum dried CDAc in aqueous medium does not show the complete regeneration of fluorescence. Thus a certain proportion of fluorescence of CDs is permanently lost upon acetone interaction (Fig. S9). This sort of quenching, due to of radiative recombination of excitons, has also been observed for acetone like molecule, benzophenone. The detection of acetone depends on the quenching efficiency of acetone as electron donor. In the present case acetone exploiting its electron donating capability forms an imine bond with the existing surface passivating group (−NH2) and is able to cause effective electronic interaction [52]. Thus the as synthesized CDs become effective for acetone sensing. The overall quenching procedure is represented schematically in Fig. 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different precursor on sensing
ce:section.ce:para: In the metabolic pathway of DA synthesis follows the path: l-phenylalanine→l-tyrosine→l-DOPA→dopamine (DA); the first three compounds are also considered as the co-precursors along with cys. Astonishingly when l-phenylalanine and l-tyrosine are used as co-precursors along with cys in the aqueous medium at pH=12, the produced product shows enhanced fluorescence intensity in the presence of acetone ( Fig. 8). This may be due to the better surface passivation by acetone and release of hydrogen bonding [54]. But this type of passivation does not work for the l-DOPA-cys and DA-cys system under the prescribed experimental conditions. The same reason of fluorescence quenching is applicable for l-DOPA-cys system. Thus the CDs prepared from the precursors l-DOPA and cys in alkaline medium (CD′) can also be used for acetone detection. CD′s have an emission maximum at 394nm but the intensity of emission is lower compared to that of our as-prepared CDs (Fig. S10). Also the sensitivity level is lower than CDs, prepared from DA and cys. CD′ can estimate acetone in water down to a [acetone]:[water]=8.75×10−6:1M level ( Fig. 9). Hence the CDs from DA and cys serve as better acetone detector.

The CDs show high fluorescence intensity when redispersed in DMSO i.e. DMSO does not cause fluorescence quenching of the as-prepared CDs. There is a report of fluorescence quenching of Au(I)@(Ag2/Ag3)-thiolate giant cluster by DMSO, while acetone enhances the fluorescence of the giant cluster [53]. Thus different solvents have varied effects on different fluorophores. The CD solution was vacuum dried and then the dry mass was dispersed in 3mL DMSO. The fluorescent solution obtained on redispersion of CD in DMSO is termed as CDDMSO. Then the effect of acetone is observed for CDDMSO from the fluorescence studies as a function of added volume of acetone. The result of acetone addition is expressed in terms of molar ratio i.e., [DMSO]/[Acetone]. Addition of acetone in this CDDMSO causes effective quenching of the fluorescence. The quenching of fluorescence can be distinguished down to 1:10−7 M ratio of [DMSO]:[Acetone] ( Fig. 10). Thus acetone contamination in DMSO can also be estimated accurately using the as prepared CD also.

Before using the as synthesized CDs for cell imaging, cytocompatibility of the CDs have been verified. An authentic MTT assay has been conducted for assessing the cytocompatibility of the CDs. CD and DMEM are mixed in different proportions and pH has been adjusted for the study. Being carbonaceous in origin the material is observed to have bioactive nature (more cell growth than control) as expected. As represented in Fig. 11A, in 72h MTT assay, the maximum cell growth has been observed at the concentration in which CDs are used for cellular imaging (40%). The cell viability is around 140% with respect to control. But it has been observed that even with 2.5 times concentration of the requirement also viability is more than the control. However there is a concentration dependent drop in viability. The cell viability of the experimental solution i.e. CD solution, is higher than the control solution (without CDs). The major reason behind such cell viability could be that one of the precursors of the CDs is cys, an amino acid, which is bioactive in nature. From the FTIR results it is observed that the CDs are rich in functional groups like –COO−, –NH2 which attributes to strong cell attachment capability and also higher cell viability. Therefore CDs can easily like bioactive surfaces and provide higher cell viability than control.

The bright fluorescent CDs find their applications in cell imaging. CD uptake and bioimaging of the cells are conducted via fluorescence microscopy. The CDs are allowed to transfect the cells for 6 hours, thereafter the imaging has been conducted (Fig. 11A). In this experiment no external transfection agent, specific ligand etc. has been used. The ultra-small CDs (2–3nm) enter the cells presumably via electric charge gradient or passive diffusion through the ion channels and aquaporins of the cell membrane. Using ethidium bromide staining, the dead cells are selectively stained (Fig. 11B. b). However a clear differentiation has been observed in CD stained cells (Fig. 11B. a) and ethidium bromide stained cells. No overlap has been observed in between these two frames. Therefore it can be concluded that CDs have selectivity over living cells. Live cells’ membrane and structure has been observed to remain intact (Fig. 11B. c). In the merged micrograph (Fig. 11B. d) certain cells are observed where cells are having a faint zone of overlap between stain of CD and ethidium bromide. As the pictures are taken without cell fixation (live cell imaging), therefore some cells have just died. As the dead cells stained with CDs do not fluoresce, we presume that the particles may come out of the ruptured cells. Again it is also seen that at the acidic pH the CDs lose the fluorescence. Generally the dead cells have a pH of 4–5 [55]. Hence for dead cells the CDs become nonfluorescent. Thus there is another possibility that the CDs do not come out from the dead cells but they become nonfluorescent inside the dead cell. Therefore this material can be useful for live cell imaging in long term and can also become a novel and economical alternative to the classic “Live-Dead” assay.

In summary, we have successfully prepared CDs from simple precursor compounds through a cost effective and simple protocol employing MHT treatment. The as prepared CDs have been proved to be very efficient in detecting trace amount of acetone contamination in water as well as in DMSO solvent systems. Also the CDs, with extremely satisfactory cell viability, happen to be very good fluorescent probe for live cell imaging and do not percolate through dead cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to the UGC, DST, NST, CSIR (Ref. No. 17-06/2012(i)EU-V) New Delhi, India, and the IIT Kharagpur for financial assistance.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2015.12.047.
